back_translation: At that time, a great outcry arose from the people and their wives against their fellow Jews.
Some were saying, "We and our sons and daughters are many. We must get grain to eat and to stay alive."
Others were saying, "We are mortgaging our fields, our vineyards, and our houses to get grain during the famine."
Still others were saying, "We have borrowed money to pay the king's tax on our fields and vineyards.

Now the flesh of us is the same as the flesh of our brothers, our children are the same as their children, yet we are forcing our sons and daughters to become slaves. Some of our daughters are already enslaved, but we have no power, because our fields and vineyards belong to others."
When I heard their outcry and these words, I became very angry.
I consulted with my own heart, then I rebuked the nobles and the officials, saying to them, "You are exacting interest from your own brothers!" So I called a great assembly to confront them.

I said to them, "We have done our best to redeem our Jewish brothers who were sold to foreigners, but now you yourselves are selling your own brothers, that they may be sold back to us again!" They were silent, finding nothing to say.
I continued saying, "What you are doing is not good. Should you not walk in the fear of our God, to avoid the reproach of the foreign nations who are our enemies?
Both I, my brothers, and my own servants have also lent them money and grain. Please, let us stop this charging of interest.
Please restore to them today their fields, vineyards, olive groves, and houses, along with the one-percent of money, grain, new wine, and oil that you have been charging them."
They replied, "We will restore it, and will demand nothing from them. We will do as you say." Then I summoned the priests and made them swear to do as they had promised.

I also shook out the folds of my robe, and said, "May God thus shake out every man who does not keep this word, out of his house and from his possessions; may such a one be shaken out and have nothing left!" The whole assembly said, "Amen," and praised the LORD. The people did according to this word.

Furthermore, from the day that King Artaxerxes appointed me to be governor in the land of Judah, from his twentieth year until his thirty-second year — twelve years total — neither I nor my brothers ate the food allotted to the governor.

The former governors who were before me had heavily burdened the people, taking from them food and wine, plus forty shekels of silver. Their servants also oppressed the people. But I did not do this, because of the fear of God.
On the contrary, I devoted myself to the work of this wall, and all my servants gathered there for the work; we did not acquire any land.
At my table there were one hundred fifty Jews and officials, besides those who came to us from the surrounding nations.

Each day, one ox, six choice sheep, and many birds were prepared for me, and every ten days an abundance of all kinds of wine was provided. Yet with all this I did not demand the food allotted to the governor, because the labor was too heavy upon this people.
O my God, remember me favorably for all that I have done for this people.
